sentences.base_language: My partner and I go to a Korean BBQ restaurant.
sentences.target_language: 파트너랑 같이 한국식 바비큐 식당에 가요.
sentences.romanized: Pateunerrang gachi hanguksik babikyu sikdange gayo.
sentences.literal: Partner-with together Korean-style BBQ restaurant[at] go.
sentences.grammarNote:
**랑/이랑** (rang/irang) = 'with / and', used casually: 파트너랑 같이 (together with my partner).
The more formal equivalents are **와/과** (wa/gwa); all three mean 'and/with' between nouns.
**같이** (gachi) = 'together', interchangeable with **함께** (hamkke) — 함께 is slightly more formal.
The destination particle **에** marks where the subject is going: 식당에 가요 (go to the restaurant).

sentences.base_language: We make a reservation in advance.
sentences.grammarNote:
**미리** (miri) = 'in advance / beforehand' — a common adverb for planning ahead.
**예약** (yeyak, 豫約) = reservation/booking. The verb is **예약하다** or the compound **예약을 하다** (to make a reservation).
**을** is the object marker after 예약 (ending in consonant ㄱ). Compare: 예약해요 (shorthand) vs. 예약을 해요 (with explicit object marker — both correct).
Making reservations (예약) is common at popular Korean restaurants, especially for grilling spots.

sentences.base_language: The server brings us the menu.
sentences.grammarNote:
**가져다 주다** (gajyeoda juda) = 'to bring (something) to someone' — a compound of 가져다 (bring over) + 주다 (give/do for).
This is more specific than 가져오다 (to bring here): 가져다 주다 emphasises delivering it as a service.
**직원** (jigwon) = staff/employee. In restaurant settings you might also hear **종업원** (jongeobwon) or simply call out **저기요!** (excuse me!).
The subject marker **이** follows 직원 (ends in consonant ㄴ).

하고 (hago) is a casual connector between nouns meaning 'and': 갈비하고 삼겹살 (galbi and samgyeopsal). It is interchangeable with the more written 와/과, and the colloquial (이)랑. All three mean 'and' between nouns.

sentences.base_language: The server lights the grill at our table.
sentences.target_language: 직원이 테이블에 있는 그릴에 불을 켜요.
sentences.romanized: Jigwoni teibeule inneun geuril-e bureul kyeoyo.
sentences.literal: Staff[subj] table[at] existing grill[at] fire[obj] turns-on.
sentences.grammarNote:
**불을 켜다** (bureul kyeoda) = 'to light a fire / turn on a flame' — 불 (fire/flame) + 켜다 (to turn on/light).
The opposite is **불을 끄다** (bureul kkeuda) = 'to turn off the fire'. This pair — 켜다/끄다 — applies to lights and flames alike.
**에 있는** after 테이블 is a relative clause: 테이블에 있는 그릴 = 'the grill that is at the table'.
In Korean BBQ restaurants, tableside grilling is central — the server typically manages the fire and turns the meat.

sentences.base_language: The meat sizzles on the grill.
sentences.grammarNote:
**지글지글** (jigeuljigeul) is an onomatopoeic word imitating the sizzling sound of meat on a grill — Korean is rich in such expressive sound words.
**위에서** (wieso) = 'on top of / from on top of' — 위 (above/top) + 에서 (location of action).
**구워지다** (guwojida) = 'to be grilled / to get cooked' — the passive-feeling form of 굽다 (to grill), formed with 지다 (to become). So 구워져요 = 'it is being grilled / it gets grilled.'

sentences.base_language: I wrap the meat in a lettuce leaf.
sentences.grammarNote: -아서/어서 connects two actions where the first is the means for the second: 싸서 먹다 = 'wrap and (then) eat'. This is different from -고 because the first action and second action are more tightly linked — you can't do the second without the first.

**맛있다** (masitda) = 'to be delicious' — 맛 (taste) + 있다 (to have/exist). Literally 'has taste.'
Its opposite is **맛없다** (madeobda) = 'to be tasteless / bland.'
**너무** (neomu) literally means 'too much / excessively' but in casual Korean it is widely used as an intensifier meaning 'so / really': 너무 맛있어요 = 'it's so delicious!'
**정말** (jeongmal) = 'really/truly' — stacking 정말 and 너무 together adds extra enthusiasm.

sentences.base_language: We also order a bottle of soju.
sentences.grammarNote: 병 (byeong) is the counter for bottles: 한 병 (one bottle), 두 병 (two bottles). Korean uses different counters depending on the category of object. Learning common counters — 개 (things), 잔 (cups), 병 (bottles), 명 (people) — is essential.

sentences.base_language: We stay at the restaurant for a long time.
sentences.grammarNote:
**에서** (eseo) marks the location where an action takes place: 식당에서 있다 (be at the restaurant).
Contrast with **에** which marks destination or static location of existence: 식당에 가다 (go to the restaurant).
**오래** (orae) = 'for a long time' — an adverb of duration.
Staying long at a restaurant is culturally common in Korea — staff rarely rush customers, making 오래 있다 a very natural phrase.

**멋지다** (meotjida) = 'to be wonderful / stylish / great' — a common adjective of admiration.
The adjective **멋진** is the modifier form: 멋진 저녁 (a wonderful evening).
**이었어요** (ieosseoyo) is the past tense of the copula 이다 (to be): 저녁이었어요 = 'it was an evening (of that kind).'
Note the pronunciation: 멋진 is often said as [멋찐] due to consonant assimilation — the ㅈ tenses after ㄷ.
